item.title: All about Raft
item.description: Introduction Raft is a distributed consensus algorithm, which was posed as an easier to understand alternative to Paxos. The Raft consensus algorithm works on replication of state machines, which is in itself broken down into three separate but concomitant tasks: Leader Election Log Replication Safety In addition to these, cluster membership changes are also accounted for in the original paper. In this blog post, we will go through the process of analyzing each of these avenues, and writing a very simple replicated state machine in Python with a custom implementation of Raft and run it across some docker containers.

item.title: Working With Rust Channels
item.description: Channels are an abstraction for sharing data between concurrently-running threads. For an example, you have a mapreduce system, and map and reduce threads are running parallelly. Each mapped output needs to be reduced using a reducer thread. Instead of passing data to a shared memory, which would require locks, you could just pass data to a channel and it would be received by the corresponding receiver thread. Using [[Go]]’s language documentation,

item.title: Namespaces in Linux
item.description: Namespaces decide what subset of resources the processes running on a system can access or “see”. With namespaces, we can have isolated mountpoints, PID spaces (so that two processes on the OS can think their PID is the same), hostnames and network interfaces isolated to processes. These resources may have a different mapping on the host system. For an example, the first process running inside a PID namespace might think that its PID is 1 but in the host system, it is actually mapped to some other PID, like 1234.
